$$C(x,y) = \left[ R(x,y),\ G(x,y),\ B(x,y) \right]$$

where each channel is quantized (typically over **8 bits**, giving $2^8 = 256$ intensity levels). The total number of distinct colours in a 24-bit RGB system:
$$N_{\text{colours}} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Mix coloured flashlights (not paints!): shine a Red light and a Green light on the same white wall — the overlap region appears **Yellow**. Add Blue to that and the centre becomes **White**. RGB is the model of **light addition**, which is why displays use it; printers, however, use subtractive models like **CMYK** (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black).

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Colours (RGB):** Red, Green, Blue
> **Secondary Colours:** Cyan = G + B, Magenta = R + B, Yellow = R + G, White = R + G + B
> **Achromatic axis:** Grayscale (R = G = B)

### Safe RGB Colour Definitions (Web Standard)

| Colour Name | R | G | B |
|---|---|---|---|
| Black | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Red | 255 | 0 | 0 |
| Lime (Green) | 0 | 255 | 0 |
| Blue | 0 | 0 | 255 |
| Yellow | 255 | 255 | 0 |
| Cyan | 0 | 255 | 255 |
| Magenta | 255 | 0 | 255 |
| White | 255 | 255 | 255 |
| Gray (mid) | 128 | 128 | 128 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the Mach Band Effect

### Lateral Inhibition — The Underlying Mechanism
The retina is composed of photoreceptors (rods and cones) connected through a **horizontal network of neurons** that perform **lateral inhibition**. When a photoreceptor $P_i$ receives light, it sends an excitatory signal to its associated bipolar/ganglion cell but simultaneously sends **inhibitory signals to the neighbouring cells**.

This results in a **Difference-of-Gaussians (DoG)** receptive field profile, mathematically given by:
$$h(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma_e \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma_e^2}\right) - \frac{w}{\sigma_i \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

where:
- $\sigma_e$ = standard deviation of the **excitatory** Gaussian (centre)
- $\sigma_i$ = standard deviation of the **inhibitory** Gaussian (surround), with $\sigma_i > \sigma_e$
- $w$ = relative weight of inhibition
- Typical ratio: $\sigma_i / \sigma_e \approx 3$ to $5$

The **perceived brightness** $B_p(x)$ at any point is then the convolution of the actual intensity profile $I(x)$ with this receptive field:
$$B_p(x) = I(x) * h(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\tau)\ h(x - \tau)\ d\tau$$

### Why Do Mach Bands Form?
- At a **step edge** in luminance, the DoG operator produces a transient overshoot (bright band) and undershoot (dark band).
- These overshoots/undershoots are **perceptual, not physical** — the actual intensity profile is smooth/linear.
- This is mathematically identical to applying an **unsharp mask** to a digital image.

---

## 2.2 RGB Model — Theoretical Analysis

### Mathematical Representation
Each colour in the RGB model can be expressed as a 3-D vector in a **unit cube**:
$$\mathbf{c} = r\mathbf{\hat{R}} + g\mathbf{\hat{G}} + b\mathbf{\hat{B}}, \quad r,g,b \in [0,1]$$

where $\mathbf{\hat{R}}, \mathbf{\hat{G}}, \mathbf{\hat{B}}$ are the basis vectors along the axes of the RGB cube.

### CIE 1931 Standard Reference Primaries
Although displays use *physical* RGB primaries, the **CIE** defines standard primaries at:
- R: $\lambda_R = 700.0$ nm
- G: $\lambda_G = 546.1$ nm
- B: $\lambda_B = 435.8$ nm

### Conversion: RGB to Grayscale (Luminance)
The perceived brightness (luminance) of an RGB pixel is computed via the **ITU-R BT.601** standard:
$$Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unequal coefficients reflect the human eye's higher sensitivity to green (≈ 58.7 %) compared to red (≈ 29.9 %) and blue (≈ 11.4 %).

### Conversion: RGB to Normalized $r,g,b$ (Chromaticity)
$$r = \frac{R}{R+G+B}, \quad g = \frac{G}{R+G+B}, \quad b = \frac{B}{R+G+B}$$

with the constraint $r + g + b = 1$ (only two are independent — **chromaticity diagram** concept).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation / Units | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visible spectrum | $\lambda$ | nm | $380 \le \lambda \le 780$ |
| 2 | RGB pixel vector | $C = [R, G, B]^T$ | 8-bit integer (per channel) | $0 \le R,G,B \le 255$ |
| 3 | Number of RGB colours (24-bit) | $N = 2^{24}$ | dimensionless | $N = 16{,}777{,}216$ |
| 4 | Hex colour code | $\#RRGGBB$ | hexadecimal | $00$ to $FF$ per pair |
| 5 | Luminance (BT.601) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | 8-bit | $0 \le Y \le 255$ |
| 6 | Luminance (BT.709 HDTV) | $Y = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ | normalized | $0 \le Y \le 1$ |
| 7 | Normalized chromaticity | $r = R/(R+G+B)$ | dimensionless | $0 \le r \le 1$ |
| 8 | Sum constraint | $r + g + b = 1$ | dimensionless | — |
| 9 | Cyan (secondary) | $C = (0, 255, 255)$ | additive | $R+G+B$ |
| 10 | Magenta (secondary) | $M = (255, 0, 255)$ | additive | $R+B$ |
| 11 | Yellow (secondary) | $Y = (255, 255, 0)$ | additive | $R+G$ |
| 12 | White (full intensity) | $W = (255, 255, 255)$ | additive | $R+G+B$ |
| 13 | DoG receptive field | $h(x) = G_e(x) - w\cdot G_i(x)$ | normalized kernel | $\int h\,dx = 0$ |
| 14 | Perceived brightness | $B_p(x) = I(x) * h(x)$ | convolution | — |
| 15 | Mach overshoot magnitude | $\Delta B \propto \dfrac{d^2 I}{dx^2}$ | second derivative | proportional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Mach Band modelling informs **edge detection (Sobel, Prewitt, Laplacian of Gaussian)**, **image sharpening (unsharp masking)**, and **retina-inspired neuromorphic cameras**. RGB modelling is foundational to **display engineering, digital photography, medical imaging (DICOM), and computer vision**.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Application Domains

| Domain | Application of Mach Band | Application of RGB |
|---|---|---|
| Medical Imaging | Enhanced tumour boundary detection | Endoscopy, MRI colour overlays |
| Remote Sensing | Land-cover edge extraction | Multispectral satellite imagery |
| Computer Vision | Canny edge detection | Object recognition, segmentation |
| Display Tech | HDR local dimming algorithms | OLED/LCD subpixel rendering |
| Photography | Local tone mapping | Raw sensor demosaicing |
| Printing | — | CMYK conversion (subtractive counterpart) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mach Band Response to a Linear Ramp

### Problem Setup
Consider a 1-D luminance profile $I(x)$ that is a **ramp function** (constant slope) transitioning from intensity $I_1$ to intensity $I_2$ over a finite edge width $w$. We want to derive the perceived brightness response $B_p(x)$.

### Step 1: Define the Input Intensity Profile
Let the intensity profile be approximated as:
$$I(x) = I_1 + \frac{(I_2 - I_1)}{w}\,x, \quad x \in [0, w]$$

Outside this interval, $I(x)$ is constant at $I_1$ (for $x < 0$) or $I_2$ (for $x > w$).

### Step 2: Define the Lateral Inhibition Kernel (DoG)
Using the Difference-of-Gaussians model:
$$h(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_e}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma_e^2}\right) - \frac{w_i}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_i}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

For simplicity, normalize the kernel so that:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(x)\ dx = 0$$

This ensures that **constant luminance regions yield constant perceived brightness** (no banding in flat areas).

### Step 3: Compute the Perceived Response via Convolution
The perceived brightness is:
$$B_p(x) = I(x) * h(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\tau)\ h(x - \tau)\ d\tau$$

### Step 4: Take the Derivative Trick
Because convolution is linear and the derivative of a convolution equals the convolution of either function with the derivative of the other:
$$\frac{d B_p}{dx} = \frac{d I}{dx} * h(x) = I(x) * \frac{d h}{dx}$$

Since $\frac{d I}{dx}$ is constant in the ramp region (call it $k$), we get:
$$\frac{d B_p}{dx} = k \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(x-\tau)\ d\tau = k \cdot H(x)$$

where $H(x) = \int h(u) du$ is the cumulative (step-response) of the kernel.

### Step 5: Integrate Back to Obtain $B_p(x)$
$$B_p(x) = B_p(0) + k \int_{0}^{x} H(\tau)\ d\tau$$

Since $H(\tau)$ is **not constant** (it is the integral of a DoG, hence an S-shaped curve with overshoot/undershoot), $B_p(x)$ will exhibit:
- An **overshoot** (perceived brighter than $I_2$) near $x = 0$ (rising edge)
- An **undershoot** (perceived darker than $I_1$) near $x = w$ (falling edge)

### Step 6: Identify the Overshoot Magnitude
The peak overshoot magnitude is proportional to the **second spatial derivative** of $I(x)$:
$$\Delta B_{\max} \approx -\sigma_e^2 \cdot \frac{d^2 I}{dx^2}$$

For a true step edge (ideal), $\dfrac{d^2 I}{dx^2}$ is a Dirac delta, producing the strongest possible Mach band.

### Step 7: Conclusion
> The Mach band effect is a **deterministic, mathematical consequence** of convolving any non-linear intensity gradient with the retina's DoG receptive field. It is **not random noise** — it is a feature-extraction operation performed by the human visual system prior to conscious perception.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: RGB Colour Computation

### Problem
An image sensor captures a pixel where the red channel registers 180, the green channel 90, and the blue channel 220 (each on the 0–255 scale).
Compute:
1. The luminance (BT.601)
2. The normalized chromaticity coordinates $(r, g, b)$
3. The hex colour code

### Step 1: Compute Luminance
$$Y = 0.299 \times 180 + 0.587 \times 90 + 0.114 \times 220$$

Computing each term:
$$Y_R = 0.299 \times 180 = 53.82$$
$$Y_G = 0.587 \times 90 = 52.83$$
$$Y_B = 0.114 \times 220 = 25.08$$

Summing:
$$Y = 53.82 + 52.83 + 25.08 = 131.73$$

Rounded to nearest integer:
$$Y \approx 132$$

### Step 2: Compute Sum of Channels
$$S = R + G + B = 180 + 90 + 220 = 490$$

### Step 3: Compute Normalized Chromaticity
$$r = \frac{180}{490} = 0.3673$$
$$g = \frac{90}{490} = 0.1837$$
$$b = \frac{220}{490} = 0.4490$$

**Sanity check:** $r + g + b = 0.3673 + 0.1837 + 0.4490 = 1.0000$ ✓

### Step 4: Convert to Hexadecimal Code
Convert each channel to hex:
$$180_{10} = B4_{16} \quad (\text{since } 11\times16 + 4 = 180)$$
$$90_{10} = 5A_{16} \quad (\text{since } 5\times16 + 10 = 90)$$
$$220_{10} = DC_{16} \quad (\text{since } 13\times16 + 12 = 220)$$

Therefore the hex colour code is:
$$\#B45ADC$$

### Step 5: Identify the Resulting Colour Visually
Since Blue (220) and Red (180) dominate while Green (90) is low, the colour is a **light purple / lavender / orchid shade**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RGB Channel Mixing Reference

| Combination | Resulting Colour | Decimal RGB | Hex Code | Sum |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R alone | Red | (255, 0, 0) | #FF0000 | 255 |
| G alone | Lime | (0, 255, 0) | #00FF00 | 255 |
| B alone | Blue | (0, 0, 255) | #0000FF | 255 |
| R + G | Yellow | (255, 255, 0) | #FFFF00 | 510 |
| G + B | Cyan | (0, 255, 255) | #00FFFF | 510 |
| R + B | Magenta | (255, 0, 255) | #FF00FF | 510 |
| R + G + B | White | (255, 255, 255) | #FFFFFF | 765 |
| $\tfrac{1}{2}(R+G+B)$ | Mid Gray | (128, 128, 128) | #808080 | 384 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical insight:** The luminance contribution of green is approximately **5× that of blue** (because $0.587 / 0.114 \approx 5.15$). This is why green-dominant images appear perceptually brighter than red- or blue-dominant images of identical digital energy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Mach Band vs. True Edge Enhancement

| Property | Mach Band (Perceptual) | Unsharp Mask (Digital) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Retinal lateral inhibition | Convolution with sharpening kernel |
| Location | Edges of luminance gradients | Everywhere (or weighted regions) |
| Reversible | Yes (brain adaptation) | Yes (subtract mask back) |
| Computational cost | Biological (free) | $O(N^2 k^2)$ per kernel size $k$ |
| Mathematical basis | $I(x) * h_{DoG}(x)$ | $I_{sharp} = I + \alpha(I - I_{blur})$ |
| Applications | Vision science, perception | Image enhancement, pre-processing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mach Band — Lateral Inhibition Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Photoreceptor 0] -->|excitatory| B0[Ganglion Cell 0]
    A0 -->|inhibitory signal minus| B1[Ganglion Cell 1]
    A0 -->|inhibitory signal minus| B2[Ganglion Cell 2]
    A1[Photoreceptor 1] -->|excitatory| B1
    A1 -->|inhibitory signal minus| B0
    A1 -->|inhibitory signal minus| B2
    A2[Photoreceptor 2] -->|excitatory| B2
    A2 -->|inhibitory signal minus| B0
    A2 -->|inhibitory signal minus| B1
    B0 --> C0[Perceived Signal at x minus 1]
    B1 --> C1[Perceived Signal at x]
    B2 --> C2[Perceived Signal at x plus 1]
```

## 4.2 Mach Band — Perceived vs. Actual Intensity Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actual Intensity Profile: Linear Ramp] --> B[Convolution with DoG Kernel h of x]
    B --> C[Perceived Brightness Profile Bp of x]
    C --> D[Overshoot: Bright Mach Band Near Rising Edge]
    C --> E[Undershoot: Dark Mach Band Near Falling Edge]
    C --> F[Linear Region: Matches Physical Intensity]
```

## 4.3 RGB Cube — Geometric Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RGBCube [RGB Color Cube Unit Volume 0 to 1 per axis]
        direction TB
        O[Origin Black: 0,0,0]
        RAxis[Red Axis: 1,0,0]
        GAxis[Green Axis: 0,1,0]
        BAxis[Blue Axis: 0,0,1]
        YAxis[Yellow: 1,1,0]
        CAxis[Cyan: 0,1,1]
        MAxis[Magenta: 1,0,1]
        WAxis[White: 1,1,1]
    end
    O --- RAxis
    O --- GAxis
    O --- BAxis
    RAxis --- YAxis
    RAxis --- MAxis
    GAxis --- YAxis
    GAxis --- CAxis
    BAxis --- CAxis
    BAxis --- MAxis
    YAxis --- WAxis
    CAxis --- WAxis
    MAxis --- WAxis
```

## 4.4 RGB Image Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene Light Source] --> B[Bayer Pattern Sensor CFA]
    B --> C[Red Channel Array]
    B --> D[Green Channel Array]
    B --> E[Blue Channel Array]
    C --> F[Demosaicing Interpolation]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Full Resolution RGB Image]
    G --> H[Display Device LCD or OLED]
    G --> I[Storage JPEG or PNG]
    G --> J[Compression Subsampling YCbCr]
```

## 4.5 Visualisation: 1-D Mach Band Response Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ActualInput [Actual Intensity Profile]
        A1[Low Intensity Plateau] --> A2[Linear Ramp Region] --> A3[High Intensity Plateau]
    end
    subgraph PerceivedOutput [Perceived Response after Lateral Inhibition]
        B1[Match Actual] --> B2[Overshoot Peak Above Plateau] --> B3[Linear Region] --> B4[Undershoot Trough Below Plateau] --> B5[Match Actual]
    end
    ActualInput -.->|convolution with DoG| PerceivedOutput
```
