# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Wiener Filter from MMSE Criterion

**Goal:** Find $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) G(u,v)$ that minimizes:

$$E\Big[\vert F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)\vert^{2}\Big]$$

### Step 1 — Substitute the estimator structure
The orthogonality principle of linear MMSE estimation states that the error $F - \hat{F}$ must be uncorrelated with every observation $G$. In the frequency domain, with $G = HF + N$ and assuming $F \perp N$:

$$E\Big[(F - WG) G^{*}\Big] = 0$$

### Step 2 — Expand
$$E\Big[F G^{*}\Big] = W\, E\Big[G G^{*}\Big]$$

$$\Rightarrow \;\; S_{fg}(u,v) = W(u,v)\, S_{gg}(u,v)$$

### Step 3 — Express the cross- and auto-spectra
Since $G = HF + N$ and $F, N$ uncorrelated, with zero means:

$$S_{fg} = E\big[F(HF+N)^{*}\big] = E\big[F(F^{*}H^{*})\big] = S_{f} H^{*}$$

$$S_{gg} = E\big[(HF+N)(HF+N)^{*}\big] = S_{f} \vert H\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Wiener filter

$$W(u,v) = \frac{S_{fg}}{S_{gg}} = \frac{H^{*} S_{f}}{S_{f}\vert H\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $G(u,v)$:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)\, S_{f}(u,v)}{S_{f}(u,v)\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v)}\; G(u,v)}$$

This is the canonical **Wiener filter** form. The **inverse filter** is recovered in the limit $S_{\eta} \to 0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Constrained Least-Squares Filter via Lagrange Multiplier

**Goal:** Solve

$$\min_{\hat{f}} \Vert Q\hat{f} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^{2} = \Vert \eta \Vert^{2}$$

### Step 1 — Build the Lagrangian
$$\mathcal{L}(\hat{f},\gamma) = \hat{f}^{T} Q^{T} Q \hat{f} + \gamma \Big[(g - H\hat{f})^{T}(g - H\hat{f}) - \eta^{T}\eta\Big]$$

### Step 2 — Take gradient w.r.t. $\hat{f}$ and set to zero
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{f}} = 2Q^{T}Q\hat{f} - 2\gamma H^{T}(g - H\hat{f}) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange

$$2Q^{T}Q\hat{f} + 2\gamma H^{T}H\hat{f} - 2\gamma H^{T}g = 0$$

$$(Q^{T}Q + \gamma H^{T}H)\hat{f} = \gamma H^{T}g$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\hat{f}$ (spatial form)

$$\boxed{\hat{f} = \big(H^{T}H + \gamma Q^{T}Q\big)^{-1} H^{T} g}$$

### Step 5 — Move to the frequency domain
For block-circulant $H$ and $Q$, the DFT diagonalizes them:

$$H \;\longrightarrow\; H(u,v), \quad Q \;\longrightarrow\; Q(u,v)$$

The matrix inversion becomes a scalar division at each frequency:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[\frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + \gamma \vert Q(u,v)\vert^{2}}\right] G(u,v)$$

### Step 6 — Connection to the Wiener filter
If we set:

$$\gamma \vert Q(u,v)\vert^{2} = \frac{S_{\eta}(u,v)}{S_{f}(u,v)}$$

then the CLS filter becomes *exactly* the Wiener filter. The CLS form is preferred in practice because $\gamma$ is **a single scalar**, not a full PSD ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bridge to KTU Questions:** The CLS filter is the answer whenever the question says "the noise PSD and image PSD are unknown but the noise variance is known" — i.e., we tune $\gamma$ iteratively.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Inverse Filter for Uniform Linear Motion Blur

**Goal:** Derive $H(u,v)$ for uniform linear motion along the $x$-axis.

### Step 1 — Write the motion-blur integral

$$g(x,y) = \int_{0}^{T} f\!\left(x - vt,\; y\right) dt$$

### Step 2 — Take the 2D Fourier transform (w.r.t. $x$ and $y$)
Using the shift property $\mathcal{F}\{f(x-x_{0},y)\} = F(u,v)\,e^{-j2\pi u x_{0}}$:

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \int_{0}^{T} e^{-j2\pi u\,v t}\, dt$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the time integral
Let $a = vT$ and substitute $\tau = vt$:

$$\int_{0}^{T} e^{-j2\pi u v t} dt = \left[\frac{e^{-j2\pi u v t}}{-j2\pi u v}\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{1 - e^{-j2\pi u a}}{j2\pi u v}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $T/a$ to express in terms of $a$:

$$= \frac{T}{j2\pi u a}\left(1 - e^{-j2\pi u a}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Use the identity $(1 - e^{-j\theta}) = e^{-j\theta/2}(e^{j\theta/2} - e^{-j\theta/2}) = 2j\, e^{-j\theta/2}\sin(\theta/2)$

$$1 - e^{-j2\pi u a} = 2j\,e^{-j\pi u a}\,\sin(\pi u a)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute back

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot \frac{T}{j2\pi u a}\cdot 2j\, e^{-j\pi u a}\,\sin(\pi u a)$$

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v)\cdot \frac{T\, e^{-j\pi u a}\,\sin(\pi u a)}{\pi u a}$$

### Step 6 — Identify $H(u,v)$

$$\boxed{H(u,v) = \frac{T\,\sin(\pi u a)}{\pi u a}\, e^{-j\pi u a}}$$

For the special case where $T$ is absorbed into the imaging model (or normalized to $T = 1/a$ giving unit DC gain in continuous $u$):

$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(\pi u a)}{\pi u a}\, e^{-j\pi u a}$$

### Step 7 — Inverse filter in frequency domain
Divide both sides by $H$ (when $\sin(\pi u a) \neq 0$):

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{\pi u a}{\sin(\pi u a)}\,e^{j\pi u a}\,G(u,v)$$

### Step 8 — Zeros of $H$
$H(u,v) = 0$ whenever $\sin(\pi u a) = 0$, i.e., $u = n/a$ for any non-zero integer $n$. At these frequencies the inverse filter blows up.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #2:** Students often forget the factor of $T$ in $H$. Also they may incorrectly write $e^{-j2\pi u a}$ instead of $e^{-j\pi u a}$ — the half-angle is correct because the integration limit is from $0$ to $T$, not $0$ to $2T$.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | Inverse Filter | Wiener Filter | CLS (Lagrange) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| Requires noise PSD? | No | Yes (or constant $K$) | No (only noise variance) |
| Requires $H$? | Yes (full) | Yes | Yes |
| Robust to noise? | **No** — amplifies | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Solution method | Division | Multiplication | Iterative on $\gamma$ |
| Mathematical form | $\hat F = G/H$ | $\hat F = W(u,v)G$ | $\hat F = M_\gamma(u,v)G$ |
| Limit $\gamma \to 0$ | — | — | Recovers inverse |
| Limit $\gamma \to \infty$ | — | — | Smoothest possible image |
| Best for | $H$ known, no noise | Known PSDs | Unknown PSDs, known noise variance |

---

## 3.5 Summary of All Closed-Form Restored Images

$$\hat{F}_{\text{Inverse}}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

$$\hat{F}_{\text{Wiener}}(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v)}{S_{f}(u,v)\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v)}\,G(u,v)$$

$$\hat{F}_{\text{CLS}}(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + \gamma \vert Q(u,v)\vert^{2}}\,G(u,v)$$

$$\hat{F}_{\text{Motion-Inv}}(u,v) = \frac{\pi u a}{\sin(\pi u a)}\,e^{j\pi u a}\,G(u,v)$$

These four are the **essential answers** for any KTU Part-B derivation question.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Restoration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Image f x y] --> B[Degradation H plus noise eta]
    B --> C[Observed Image g x y]
    C --> D{Choose Restoration Strategy}
    D -- Noise = 0 and H invertible --> E[Inverse Filter]
    D -- Noise PSD and Image PSD known --> F[Wiener Filter]
    D -- Noise variance known only --> G[Constrained LS via Lagrange]
    D -- Known H is motion blur --> H[Motion Inverse Filter]
    E --> I[Restored Image f hat]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Constrained Least-Squares Closed-Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: initial gamma] --> X[Compute f hat = HT H + gamma QT Q inv HT g]
    X --> R[Compute residual r = norm g minus H f hat squared]
    R --> Q{r vs noise variance}
    Q -- r less than noise variance --> U[Increase gamma]
    Q -- r greater than noise variance --> D[Decrease gamma]
    U --> X
    D --> X
    Q -- r approx equals noise variance --> OK[Converged: output f hat]
```

## 4.3 Wiener vs. CLS Conceptual Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unconstrained MMSE] --> B[Wiener Filter]
    C[Constrained LS] --> D[CLS Filter]
    B -- gamma Q T Q equals S eta over S f --> D
    D -- same substitution --> B
    B --> E{Generalized Restoration Family}
    D --> E
    F[Inverse Filter] -- noise tends to 0 --> E
```

## 4.4 Motion-Blur Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Static Scene f x y] --> B[Camera translates at velocity v for time T]
    B --> C[Smeared Image g x y]
    C --> D[2D FFT to get G u v]
    D --> E[Multiply by Inverse Filter pi u a over sin pi u a times e j pi u a]
    E --> F[2D IFFT]
    F --> G[Restored Image f hat]
```

## 4.5 Filter Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Frequency_Bands
        A1[Low Freq: H large] --> R1[All filters work]
        A2[Mid Freq: H moderate] --> R2[Wiener and CLS attenuate noise]
        A3[High Freq near zeros of H] --> R3[Inverse explodes Wiener dampens CLS fully regularized]
    end
```

## 4.6 Restoration Method Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is noise present?] --> Q2{Is noise PSD known?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Wiener Filter]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Is noise variance known?]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Use CLS Lagrange]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Assume small constant K and use Wiener]
    Q1 -- No --> Q7[Use Inverse Filter]
    Q3 --> Q8[Done]
    Q5 --> Q8
    Q6 --> Q8
    Q7 --> Q8
```
